content.text: Before modern cryptography, ciphers relied on simple transformations.

content.title: Caesar Cipher
content.content:
The **Caesar cipher** shifts each letter by a fixed amount: if 
$x$ is the numerical value of a letter (A=0, B=1, ..., Z=25), the 
encrypted letter is $E(x) = x + k \pmod{26}$.

content.text: The Caesar cipher is trivially broken: there are only 26 possible keys.

content.title: Affine Cipher
content.content:
The **affine cipher** uses $E(x) = ax + b \pmod{26}$ where 
$\gcd(a, 26) = 1$.

content.title: Frequency Analysis
content.problem:
In English text, E is the most common letter ( 12\%), followed by T, A, O, I, N.

If the most frequent letter in ciphertext is W, we guess W decrypts to E, 
yielding $k = 22 - 4 = 18$ for a Caesar cipher.

content.title: Vigenère Cipher
content.content:
The **Vigenère cipher** uses a keyword. If the keyword is KEY and 
the plaintext is ATTACK:

| A | T | T | A | C | K |
| K | E | Y | K | E | Y |
| K | X | R | K | G | I |

Each plaintext letter is shifted by the corresponding keyword letter.

content.text:
The Vigenère cipher resisted analysis for centuries, but was eventually 
broken using the **Kasiski examination** (finding repeated patterns) 
and **index of coincidence**.

content.title: Perfect Secrecy
content.content:
If the key is truly random, as long as the message, and used only once, 
the Vigenère cipher (one-time pad) is **perfectly secure**—the 
ciphertext reveals nothing about the plaintext.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
The practical difficulty is key distribution: how do sender and receiver 
securely share a key as long as every message they'll ever exchange?
